title: Stability of the Positive Mass Thm
speaker: Lan Hsuan Haung

proof_stages.label: Weak curvature-sign convexity problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins by comparing strict and weak curvature inequalities for hypersurfaces, explaining that local weak Gauss or sigma_k conditions do not force convexity and that higher-degree sigma_k analogues fail, leaving the sigma_2 scalar-curvature case as the special problem.

proof_stages.label: Scalar-curvature case and geodesic-point reduction
proof_stages.summary: For a closed embedded sufficiently smooth hypersurface in Euclidean space with nonnegative scalar curvature, the Gauss equation shows that any sign change of mean curvature must pass through the totally geodesic set M_0, whose connected components are controlled by Sacksteder's theorem and lie in affine hyperplanes with second-order contact.

proof_stages.label: Naive positive-mass cut-and-paste contradiction
proof_stages.summary: Assuming the mean curvature changes sign, one would like to cut along the hyperplane containing M_0 and glue the lower piece to a flat exterior, obtaining an asymptotically flat zero-mass space that positive-mass rigidity would force to be Euclidean, but the zero set is too irregular for a direct application.

proof_stages.label: Graphical divergence formula and nonnegative mass
proof_stages.summary: For a hypersurface written globally as a graph, the scalar curvature is an explicit divergence on the base, so integrating over expanding balls identifies the boundary flux with the ADM mass and immediately gives nonnegativity, with zero mass implying scalar flatness.

proof_stages.label: Level-set quasi-local mass and rigidity
proof_stages.summary: Extending the graphical argument to graphs outside a compact set, the inner-boundary flux is rewritten as a weighted mean-curvature integral over level sets, producing a monotone quasi-local mass whose zero value, together with a mean-curvature-vector angle inequality and the existence of convex points, forces rigidity.

proof_stages.label: Intrinsic-flat stability and graphical stability results
proof_stages.summary: The stability problem asks how asymptotically flat nonnegative-scalar-curvature manifolds with mass tending to zero converge to Euclidean space; strong global convergence fails, leading to the Sormani--Wenger and Lee--Sormani intrinsic-flat conjectural formulation and to known partial results, including the graphical case via the quasi-local mass estimates.

examples.name: Bent plane with zero curvature product
examples.note: A plane, or Euclidean space, bent in only one direction keeps one principal curvature zero, so the weak curvature condition holds while convexity or hyperplanarity fails; this forces the later use of global and rigidity hypotheses.
examples.engagement_reason: he bends a plane in one direction and checks the principal curvatures have product zero while the surface is not convex

passes.reason: A developable bent plane has one flat principal direction and saturates K=0, and is used to refute local weak convexity from K≥0.
a developable bent plane has one principal curvature identically zero, so the Gauss-curvature product is the tight boundary value 0 while it refutes weak convexity.
One principal curvature is identically zero, making K=0 a degenerate boundary case, and it refutes local K≥0 implying weak convexity.
One principal curvature is identically zero, so the surface is a degenerate boundary case K=0 used to refute local weak convexity from K≥0.
one principal curvature is identically zero so the bent plane is a degenerate K=0 boundary case used to refute local weak convexity from K>=0
A developable bent plane has one principal curvature identically zero, so K≡0 is the weak-boundary case used to refute local weak convexity.
A developable one-direction bend has one principal curvature identically zero, so it saturates K=0 while the other curvature changes sign, refuting local weak convexity.
A developable bent plane has one principal curvature identically zero, so it lies on the zero-Gauss-curvature boundary and is used to refute local weak convexity from K≥0.
Refutes local K≥0 implying weak convexity, with a developable bent plane having one principal curvature identically zero on the K=0 boundary.
A one-directional bending of a plane has an identically zero principal curvature, so zero curvature product, and is used to disprove local weak convexity from K>=0.
It refutes local nonnegative Gauss-curvature-implies-convexity and is a developable boundary case with one principal curvature identically zero.
A one-directional developable bend has one principal curvature identically zero, so the zero Gauss-curvature boundary case refutes weak convexity.

examples.name: Rotated-circle Gauss--Kronecker counterexample
examples.note: A circle is rotated in higher-dimensional Euclidean space to produce a closed hypersurface with nonnegative Gauss--Kronecker curvature but not weakly convex, and variants give the counterexamples for sigma_k with k at least 3, isolating the scalar-curvature case.
examples.engagement_reason: he constructs the example by rotating a circle about an axis and discusses choosing parameters so the Gauss--Kronecker curvature is nonnegative but convexity fails

passes.reason: The rotational hypersurface from a circle is a highly symmetric ordinary family member, used to disprove the higher-dimensional Gauss--Kronecker sign-implies-convexity claim.
the rotational hypersurface built from a circle is a highly symmetric boundary case with Gauss--Kronecker curvature only nonnegative, used to disprove the higher-dimensional convexity conclusion.
The round-circle hypersurface of revolution is highly symmetric but belongs to a standard family, and is used to refute nonnegative Gauss--Kronecker curvature implying weak convexity.
This rotationally symmetric hypersurface of revolution is used to disprove that nonnegative Gauss--Kronecker curvature forces weak convexity in higher dimensions.
the rotated circle gives a highly rotationally symmetric torus-like hypersurface with nonnegative Gauss--Kronecker curvature but not weak convexity
The circle-generated hypersurface of revolution is highly symmetric and is used to show nonnegative Gauss--Kronecker curvature need not imply weak convexity in higher dimensions.

The circle-generated hypersurface of revolution has nonnegative Gauss--Kronecker curvature with zero/parabolic loci but mixed principal-curvature signs, giving Chen--Lashof's nonconvex counterexample.
The rotated circle gives a rotationally symmetric closed hypersurface with nonnegative Gauss--Kronecker curvature but changing principal-curvature signs, refuting weak convexity in higher dimension.
Used as the Chen–Lashof higher-dimensional Gauss–Kronecker counterexample, and the rotated-circle hypersurface is rotationally symmetric but not sporadic or limiting.
The rotated-circle hypersurface is a highly rotationally symmetric torus-like example used to show nonnegative Gauss--Kronecker curvature need not imply weak convexity in higher dimensions.
It is used to disprove the higher-dimensional Gauss--Kronecker convexity analogue and is a highly rotationally symmetric torus-like hypersurface.
The rotational hypersurface from a circle is a highly symmetric closed embedded nonconvex example with nonnegative Gauss--Kronecker curvature.

examples.name: Schwarzschild graph with shrinking neck
examples.note: The Schwarzschild metric is written as a rotationally symmetric scalar-flat graph with an inner minimal boundary; reflecting it gives a nonflat scalar-flat model, and letting the mass tend to zero shows why stability cannot be formulated using overly strong global convergence.
examples.engagement_reason: he analyzes the Schwarzschild metric as a graph, describes the minimal neck, reflection, scalar flatness, and how the neck shrinks as the mass goes to zero

passes.reason: The spherically symmetric scalar-flat Schwarzschild graph has an m→0 collapsing neck and lies on the R=0 boundary, and is used to show scalar-flat graphical AF hypersurfaces need not be hyperplanes.

the spherically symmetric scalar-flat Schwarzschild graph has a neck collapsing in the zero-mass limit and shows scalar-flat or small-mass hypotheses do not force a hyperplane or strong global convergence.
The spherically symmetric scalar-flat Schwarzschild graphs approach the zero-mass boundary with a collapsing neck, and show scalar-flat graphical ends need not be Euclidean.

The rotationally symmetric scalar-flat Schwarzschild family approaches the zero-mass Euclidean limit with a collapsing neck and is used to show scalar-flat or near-zero-mass hypotheses alone do not give flatness or strong global convergence.
the spherically symmetric scalar-flat Schwarzschild graphs approach the zero-mass limit with a collapsing neck and show scalar-flat graphs need not be hyperplanes
The rotationally symmetric Schwarzschild graph is scalar-flat/equality-type, with the mass-zero limit collapsing the neck, and is used to show scalar-flat graphical ends need not be hyperplanes.
The spherically symmetric Schwarzschild graph is scalar-flat and has a minimal neck that collapses as mass tends to zero, obstructing naive scalar-flat rigidity or strong global stability.

The Schwarzschild graph is a rotationally symmetric scalar-flat/asymptotically flat model approaching the zero-mass limiting case with a shrinking neck, and is used to show scalar-flat graphical hypersurfaces need not be hyperplanes.

Shows scalar-flat graphical/asymptotically flat geometry need not be a hyperplane and models failure of strong stability, with rotational symmetry and a neck collapsing in the zero-mass boundary limit.

The Schwarzschild graph is spherically symmetric and scalar-flat, with the small-mass neck collapsing in the limit, and is used to show scalar-flat/asymptotically flat graphs need not be flat without extra hypotheses.
It shows scalar-flat graphical/asymptotically flat hypersurfaces need not be hyperplanes, while Schwarzschild is a spherically symmetric scalar-flat boundary model.
The spherically symmetric scalar-flat Schwarzschild family has a neck collapsing in the zero-mass limit and obstructs naive scalar-flat rigidity or strong stability.
